source_paper_title: Exactness and Effective Degree Bound of Lasserre’s Relaxation for Polynomial Optimization over Finite Variety
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Hua_2025, title={Exactness and Effective Degree Bound of Lasserre’s Relaxation for Polynomial Optimization over Finite Variety}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0483}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0483}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Hua, Zheng and Qu, Zheng}, year={2025}, month=jul }
context:
Section 8 (Conclusion and perspectives), page 21.

However, Assumption 1 is stronger than the regular sequence property of \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\), see Section C. Indeed, \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\) is still a regular sequence if \(\{g^\infty_1, \ldots, g^\infty_n\}\) has finitely many complex common zero locus. However, the H-basis property may fail if we only assume that \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\) is a regular sequence. It would be interesting to investigate the degree bound under the regular sequence condition only.
title: Derive effective degree bounds for Lasserre relaxations under regular-sequence constraints
background_latex:
Consider a polynomial optimization problem
\[
\min_{x\in\mathbb R^n} f(x)\quad \text{s.t.}\quad g_1(x)=\cdots=g_k(x)=0,\; h_1(x)\ge 0,\ldots,h_m(x)\ge 0,
\]
where \(f,g_1,\ldots,g_k,h_1,\ldots,h_m\in\mathbb R[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\). Let \(f^*\) denote the optimal value (assumed finite and attained when needed).

Let \(\langle g_1,\ldots,g_k\rangle\subseteq \mathbb R[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\) be the ideal generated by the equality constraints. Let \(\Sigma[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\) denote the cone of sum-of-squares (SOS) polynomials. Let \(h_0\equiv 1\), and define the quadratic module
\[
\mathrm{QM}(h_0,\ldots,h_m):=\Big\{\sum_{j=0}^m \sigma_j h_j : \sigma_j\in \Sigma[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\Big\}.
\]
For \(d\in\mathbb N\), let \(\mathbb R[x]_{\le d}\) be polynomials of degree at most \(d\). Define truncations
\[
\langle g_1,\ldots,g_k\rangle_{\le d}:=\Big\{\sum_{i=1}^k \lambda_i g_i : \lambda_i\in\mathbb R[x],\; \deg(\lambda_i g_i)\le d\Big\},
\]
\[
\mathrm{QM}(h_0,\ldots,h_m)_{\le d}:=\Big\{\sum_{j=0}^m \sigma_j h_j : \sigma_j\in\Sigma[x],\;\deg(\sigma_j h_j)\le d\Big\}.
\]
The order-\(d\) Lasserre (moment-SOS) relaxation for exactness of the SOS side can be phrased as the existence of a certificate
\[
 f-f^*\in \langle g_1,\ldots,g_k\rangle_{\le 2d}+\mathrm{QM}(h_0,\ldots,h_m)_{\le 2d}.
\]

For a polynomial \(p\in\mathbb R[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\), let \(\bar p\in\mathbb R[x_0,x_1,\ldots,x_n]\) denote its homogenization and let \(p_\infty\) be the highest-degree homogeneous part (equivalently \(p_\infty(x)=\bar p(0,x)\)). For \(g_i\), write \(\bar g_i\) for its homogenization. A sequence \(\{\bar g_1,\ldots,\bar g_n\}\subset \mathbb R[x_0,\ldots,x_n]\) is called a regular sequence if each \(\bar g_{i+1}\) is not a zero divisor modulo \(\langle \bar g_1,\ldots,\bar g_i\rangle\), and \(\langle \bar g_1,\ldots,\bar g_n\rangle\ne \mathbb R[x_0,\ldots,x_n]\).

The paper’s main effective degree bounds are derived under a stronger “no solutions at infinity” assumption on \(g_{1,\infty},\ldots,g_{n,\infty}\). The authors note that if one weakens this to only assuming \(\{\bar g_1,\ldots,\bar g_n\}\) is a regular sequence (equivalently, that \(\{g_{1,\infty},\ldots,g_{n,\infty}\}\) has finitely many common complex zeros), then a key algebraic property (an H-basis property used to control degrees) may fail, and new techniques may be required to obtain explicit degree bounds.
problem_definition_latex:
Assume \(k\ge n\) and the complex variety \(\{z\in\mathbb C^n: g_1(z)=\cdots=g_k(z)=0\}\) is finite.

Assume that the homogenized polynomials \(\bar g_1,\ldots,\bar g_n\in\mathbb R[x_0,\ldots,x_n]\) form a regular sequence (equivalently, \(g_{1,\infty},\ldots,g_{n,\infty}\) have only finitely many common complex zeros), but do not assume the stronger condition that \(g_{1,\infty}(z)=\cdots=g_{n,\infty}(z)=0\) has no solution in \(\mathbb C^n\setminus\{0\}\).

Find an explicit bound (as a function of the degrees \(\deg(f),\deg(g_1),\ldots,\deg(g_k),\deg(h_1),\ldots,\deg(h_m)\), and possibly \(n\)) on the relaxation order \(d\) that guarantees the existence of a truncated moment-SOS certificate
\[
 f-f^*\in \langle g_1,\ldots,g_k\rangle_{\le 2d}+\mathrm{QM}(h_0,\ldots,h_m)_{\le 2d},
\]
under appropriate nonsingularity/exactness hypotheses analogous to those used in the paper for the stronger “no solutions at infinity” setting.

Assume \(k\ge n\) and the complex variety \(\{z\in\mathbb C^n: g_1(z)=\cdots=g_k(z)=0\}\) is finite.

Assume that the homogenized polynomials \(\bar g_1,\ldots,\bar g_n\in\mathbb R[x_0,\ldots,x_n]\) form a regular sequence (equivalently, \(g_{1,\infty},\ldots,g_{n,\infty}\) have only finitely many common complex zeros), but do not assume the stronger condition that \(g_{1,\infty}(z)=\cdots=g_{n,\infty}(z)=0\) has no solution in \(\mathbb C^n\setminus\{0\}\).

Find an explicit bound (as a function of the degrees \(\deg(f),\deg(g_1),\ldots,\deg(g_k),\deg(h_1),\ldots,\deg(h_m)\), and possibly \(n\)) on the relaxation order \(d\) that guarantees the existence of a truncated moment-SOS certificate
\[

under appropriate nonsingularity/exactness hypotheses analogous to those used in the paper for the stronger “no solutions at infinity” setting.
open_problem_quote: However, Assumption 1 is stronger than the regular sequence property of \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\), see Section C. Indeed, \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\) is still a regular sequence if \(\{g^\infty_1, \ldots, g^\infty_n\}\) has finitely many complex common zero locus. However, the H-basis property may fail if we only assume that \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\) is a regular sequence. It would be interesting to investigate the degree bound under the regular sequence condition only.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can algorithmically generate small-degree polynomial systems where the homogenized constraints form a regular sequence but the H-basis property fails, and then test numerically/SDP-based whether low-order truncations certify optimality. However, the search space is large and verifying exact degree bounds symbolically can be expensive.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: There is no clear automatic fitness signal for candidate proofs or general degree bounds, since evaluating a proposed bound requires worst-case certification across infinite families of polynomial systems and may need symbolic algebraic geometry arguments beyond scalable automated checking.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
